proposals.title: Biome-specific nutrient limitation thresholds: Testing N and P effects on biodiversity and biomass dynamics in tundra and desert ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Global meta-analyses of nutrient enrichment effects on biodiversity are heavily biased towards temperate grasslands and aquatic systems, leaving critical knowledge gaps for biomes like tundra and deserts. These understudied ecosystems are experiencing rapid environmental change and are theoretically predicted to have distinct nutrient limitation regimes and community stability properties. Understanding their response to potential eutrophication is essential for predicting global biodiversity change and ecosystem function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tundra communities are primarily nitrogen (N)-limited but exhibit strong threshold declines in species richness with enrichment, while desert communities show co-limitation by N and phosphorus (P) with more variable biomass responses driven by individual species dynamics, as predicted by site- and species-level scaling of stability.
proposals.method: We will conduct fully factorial N and P addition field experiments across multiple sites in Arctic/alpine tundra and cold deserts. Response variables will include species richness, total biomass, and species-specific biomass tracked over time. We will analyze the data using Linear Mixed Effects Models to account for site variability, and employ Threshold Indicator Taxa Analysis (TITAN) and breakpoint regression to rigorously test for non-linear richness responses, moving beyond the temptation of assumed tipping points. Temporal stability of biomass and its spatial scaling will be decomposed into species and portfolio effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the generality of nutrient-biodiversity relationships across biomes, reducing bias in global syntheses. It will provide empirical evidence on whether concepts of thresholds and tipping points are applicable to biodiversity change in these sensitive ecosystems. The analysis will link nutrient limitation to community stability patterns, extending Hillebrand's work on the drivers of spatial scaling of stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying rigorous, time-series based threshold detection and stability-partitioning frameworks to nutrient enrichment experiments in these underrepresented biomes, moving beyond simple richness-biomass correlations to mechanistically understand community change.

proposals.title: Microbial community stability and function are governed by the spatial scaling of nitrogen and phosphorus co-limitation
proposals.motivation: Nutrient limitation fundamentally structures ecosystems, but its dynamics at the scale of microbial communities—the engines of biogeochemical cycles—are poorly resolved. Pre-2026 research on biodiversity change often uses aggregate metrics like richness, potentially missing how nutrient constraints on individual species and their interactions scale up to affect community stability and function. This gap limits our ability to predict ecosystem responses to anthropogenic nutrient loading.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and spatial scaling of N and P co-limitation in soil and planktonic microbial communities are primary drivers of local species dynamics, which in turn determine the scaling of aggregate community stability and biomass ratios.
proposals.method: We will conduct coordinated nutrient enrichment experiments (N, P, N+P) in terrestrial and aquatic microcosms, measuring microbial biomass (via PLFA/PLEA and flow cytometry), taxonomic composition (via 16S/18S rRNA amplicon sequencing), and function (respiration, extracellular enzyme activity). Time-series data on species abundances and community biomass will be analyzed using the spatial scaling of stability framework from Hillebrand et al. (pre-2026), quantifying how variance and synchrony of individual populations contribute to total community stability across treatments. Co-limitation will be assessed using statistical models (e.g., ANOVA of interaction effects) and linked to stability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will mechanistically link nutrient limitation to the scaling of biodiversity-stability relationships, moving beyond simple richness correlations. It will provide a process-based understanding of how environmental change filters through individual species responses to alter microbial ecosystem functioning.
proposals.novelty: While nutrient limitation is well-studied, applying the spatial scaling of stability framework—typically used for macro-communities—to microbial co-limitation dynamics is novel. It tests whether concepts of thresholds and tipping points are appropriate for microbial nutrient responses, aligning with Hillebrand's recent critical work.

proposals.title: Reconstructing historical nutrient limitation regimes from community biomass ratios in paleoecological time series
proposals.motivation: Understanding historical baselines and trajectories of nutrient limitation is crucial for interpreting anthropogenic biodiversity change, but direct long-term nutrient data are rare. Paleoecological and monitoring records contain rich community data (e.g., diatom, foraminifera assemblages) that reflect productivity, yet the inverse problem—inferring the limiting nutrient (N, P, Si, Fe) from these compositional shifts—remains largely unaddressed. This gap limits our ability to separate natural from human-driven nutrient dynamics in ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: Historical shifts in nutrient limitation regimes leave distinct, quantifiable signatures in the biomass ratios and size structure of fossil or long-term community assemblages, allowing for the probabilistic reconstruction of past limitation states.
proposals.method: We will compile high-resolution paleoecological (e.g., sediment cores) and monitoring time series with known community biomass or abundance data. Using a state-space modeling framework, we will treat the historical limitation regime as a hidden state. The observation model will link this state to emergent community properties, specifically cross-community biomass ratios (inspired by Hillebrand's work on ratios) and size-spectra slopes, which are known from pre-2026 meta-analyses to respond predictably to N vs. P vs. co-limitation. Model inference will be performed via Bayesian methods (e.g., Stan) to estimate posterior probabilities of limitation states over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for reconstructing nutrient history from widely available biological archives, moving beyond simple correlative approaches. It will generate testable, quantitative timelines of limitation change, offering new insights into the drivers of long-term community dynamics and stability, a core theme in Hillebrand's research.
proposals.novelty: While paleo-indicators exist for nutrient concentrations, inferring the *limiting* nutrient is an inverse problem rarely tackled. The approach is novel in applying dynamic state-space models to community biomass ratios for this purpose, directly addressing the challenge of bias in biodiversity time series highlighted in recent work.

proposals.title: Diversity-Stability Trade-offs: Quantifying the Ecosystem Service Costs of Maintaining a Single Stable State
proposals.motivation: The stability-diversity relationship is a cornerstone of ecology, often implicitly assuming that a single, stable state is optimal for ecosystem function and service provision. However, pre-2026 work, including Hillebrand's own, shows that local community dynamics are highly variable and that biodiversity change is non-linear. This raises a critical question: does forcing a system into one perceived 'stable' state come at the cost of other vital services, especially under environmental change?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ecosystems constrained to a single historical or target stable state exhibit significant trade-offs, where high stability in one aggregate property (e.g., total biomass) is achieved at the expense of reduced performance in other key ecosystem services (e.g., nutrient filtration, genetic diversity, or functional redundancy).
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term time series of aquatic communities (e.g., phytoplankton, zooplankton) where biomass and species richness are tracked alongside proxies for multiple services (production, stoichiometry, size structure). Using state-space models and multivariate stability metrics, we will quantify the covariance of service provision over time. We will then apply a novel 'stability-constraint' simulation, artificially damping species and site dynamics to maintain a constant aggregate biomass, and measure the consequent change in the variance and mean of other services using variance ratio (VR) decomposition and cross-correlation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the stability discourse from a singular goal to a multidimensional framework, directly linking community dynamics to ecosystem service portfolios. It will provide a quantitative method to assess the hidden costs of stability-oriented management, challenging the sufficiency of 'stable biomass' as a universal management target.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs among services are known, explicitly testing the trade-off imposed by the *pursuit of a single stable state* is novel. It inverts the classic paradigm by treating maximum stability not as an unconditional benefit, but as a potential constraint on adaptive capacity and multifunctionality.

proposals.title: A multidimensional classification of anthropogenic disturbances to reconcile biodiversity change metrics across scales
proposals.motivation: Current biodiversity assessments often conflate different types of anthropogenic change, leading to contradictory findings about trends in richness, biomass, and stability. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to move beyond simplistic disturbance gradients (e.g., low vs. high impact) to a framework that explicitly separates key disturbance characteristics like magnitude, frequency, spatial extent, and predictability. This is critical for interpreting the 'biased' time series and scaling patterns observed in Hillebrand's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multidimensional disturbance taxonomy, based on quantifiable axes, will explain more variance in biodiversity change metrics (e.g., richness trends, biomass ratios, stability scaling) than traditional unidimensional frameworks, and will reveal distinct thresholds for different community properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize data from long-term observational and experimental studies (e.g., nutrient enrichment, warming, habitat fragmentation) to extract quantitative measures for each disturbance dimension. Using multivariate statistical techniques, specifically Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and subsequent k-means clustering, we will define distinct disturbance regimes. We will then apply mixed-effects models to test how these classified regimes predict changes in species richness, community biomass, and the spatial scaling of stability, explicitly accounting for temporal and site-specific dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, quantitative typology for ecological disturbances, enabling more precise meta-analyses and comparisons across systems. It will directly address the challenge of 'tempting but not necessarily suitable' tipping points by linking specific disturbance configurations to measurable biodiversity outcomes, thereby refining predictions of anthropogenic change effects.
proposals.novelty: While disturbance characteristics are often discussed qualitatively, this proposal operationalizes them into a quantitative, data-driven classification system explicitly designed to resolve inconsistencies in biodiversity change metrics across spatial and temporal scales, a core challenge identified in Hillebrand's pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Quantifying ecological resilience: A novel framework adapting social-ecological system indicators to community stability
proposals.motivation: Ecological stability is a multifaceted concept, but its assessment often relies on a narrow set of metrics like species richness or biomass variability, which may miss critical aspects of resilience. Concurrently, social-ecological systems (SES) research has developed sophisticated, multi-dimensional resilience indicators (e.g., adaptive capacity, transformability, robustness) that are rarely applied to purely ecological contexts. Bridging this disciplinary gap is crucial for a more predictive understanding of how biodiversity change affects ecosystem stability in the Anthropocene.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key resilience indicators from SES theory—specifically, robustness, adaptability, and transformability—can be operationalized using purely ecological time-series data to provide a more comprehensive and diagnostic assessment of community stability than traditional variance-based metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the three core SES resilience attributes into quantifiable ecological indicators. Robustness will be measured as the inverse of compositional change (Bray-Curtis) following a perturbation. Adaptability will be quantified as the rate of functional recovery (using trait-based redundancy) post-disturbance. Transformability will be assessed as the magnitude of directional shift in community state space (via PCA) before and after a major stressor. We will test these indicators using long-term phytoplankton and zooplankton time-series data from multiple aquatic ecosystems, applying state-space models and breakpoint detection to identify perturbation events and recovery trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide ecologists with a novel, multi-dimensional toolkit to diagnose the type and magnitude of stability change in communities, moving beyond simple variance metrics. It will directly inform debates on thresholds and tipping points by offering empirically grounded, process-based resilience indicators, thereby enhancing our ability to predict biodiversity change effects on ecosystem functioning.
proposals.novelty: While SES frameworks are well-established, their deliberate translation and rigorous testing for application in purely ecological stability analysis, divorced from human governance components, is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond correlative stability metrics to propose causal, mechanism-informed indicators of resilience.

proposals.title: Trophic network motifs mediate the scaling of biodiversity effects on ecosystem functioning
proposals.motivation: Biodiversity-ecosystem functioning (BEF) research has established that species richness enhances ecosystem properties like biomass production. However, these insights largely stem from simplified, non-trophic experiments, while natural systems are characterized by complex food webs. A critical pre-2026 gap is understanding how the architecture of trophic interactions—beyond simple richness—modulates and transmits biodiversity effects across different organizational levels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and stability of biodiversity effects on aggregate community biomass are not direct functions of species richness, but are mediated by recurrent, statistically overrepresented sub-structures (motifs) within the trophic interaction network.
proposals.method: We will construct and analyze trophic networks from long-term, spatially replicated time-series data of aquatic communities (e.g., phytoplankton-zooplankton). Using established network algorithms (e.g., the `mfinder` or `igraph` packages), we will identify three- and four-species motifs (e.g., omnivory, apparent competition chains). We will then use mixed-effects models to test how the prevalence of specific motifs, alongside species richness and biomass ratios, predicts the temporal stability of total community biomass and the scaling of stability from sites to regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will move BEF theory beyond the direct richness-function paradigm by quantifying the architectural constraints imposed by trophic complexity. It will provide a mechanistic, network-based framework to explain context-dependency in biodiversity effects and reconcile empirical patterns of stability scaling observed in natural systems.
proposals.novelty: While food web ecology uses network theory, and BEF research measures richness effects, this proposal uniquely integrates the two by applying motif analysis—a tool from systems biology—to directly test how interaction patterns govern classic BEF relationships, addressing a key conceptual gap highlighted in recent syntheses.

proposals.title: Linking local trait divergence to continental diversity gradients: A macroecological test of scale-dependent biodiversity-ecosystem function relationships
proposals.motivation: Biodiversity-ecosystem function (BEF) theory is largely grounded in local-scale experiments and observations, while large-scale biogeographic patterns in phylogenetic and functional diversity are well-documented. A critical, unresolved gap is understanding how local mechanisms of trait divergence and complementarity, which drive BEF relationships, scale up to explain these continental-scale diversity gradients. Bridging this scale chasm is essential for predicting how regional species pools and biogeographic history constrain local ecosystem functioning in a changing world.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and direction of local trait-divergence BEF patterns are systematically predictable from the structure of the regional species pool, specifically its phylogenetic breadth and functional trait space, with stronger local BEF signals emerging from regions with higher biogeographic and evolutionary distinctiveness.
proposals.method: We will integrate continental-scale species distribution data (e.g., GBIF) with trait databases (e.g., TRY, FishBase) and phylogenetic trees for aquatic (fish, phytoplankton) and terrestrial (plant) taxa. For multiple regions, we will calculate regional-scale metrics of phylogenetic diversity (PD) and functional richness. We will then meta-analyze existing local BEF studies (e.g., from BioTIME) to extract effect sizes of trait divergence on ecosystem biomass or productivity. Using multi-level Bayesian hierarchical models, we will test if regional PD and functional richness predict the magnitude of these local BEF effect sizes, controlling for environmental covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical macroecological framework that mechanistically links local BEF dynamics to large-scale biogeographic and evolutionary patterns. It will move BEF research beyond a local context, offering a predictive model for how regional biodiversity loss may cascade to alter local ecosystem functioning. It directly addresses Hillebrand's recent work on spatial scaling of stability and critiques of simplistic tipping points by emphasizing continuous, scale-dependent drivers.
proposals.novelty: While scaling is recognized as a key issue, explicitly testing regional species pool attributes as predictors of local BEF relationship strength is novel. The approach uniquely combines meta-analysis of local dynamics with macroecological data synthesis to formalize the cross-scale linkage, moving from correlation to mechanistic prediction.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Biodiversity Enrichment: Quantifying the Role of Beneficial Species Influx in High-Turnover Communities
proposals.motivation: Biodiversity change is predominantly framed as net loss, yet high temporal turnover can mask complex dynamics of simultaneous extinction and colonization. Pre-2026 research, including Hillebrand's work on biased time series, highlights that observed richness increases may reflect environmental change, but the specific contribution of beneficial species influx to ecosystem structure and function remains poorly quantified. This bias towards a loss-centric view limits our understanding of potential ecological gains and resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in specific anthropogenic or successional contexts, high temporal turnover is primarily driven by the influx of functionally beneficial species, leading to net increases in community biomass and functional diversity without eroding native community integrity.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term time-series data (e.g., from freshwater plankton or coastal benthos surveys) where net richness is stable or increasing. Using a site- and species-dynamic framework, we will partition temporal beta-diversity into loss and gain components, linking gains to species traits and environmental drivers. We will employ generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) to test for non-linear thresholds in the relationship between gain-driven turnover and ecosystem metrics like biomass stability and functional richness ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to decouple beneficial species influx from loss in biodiversity assessments, moving beyond net change metrics. It will directly test the applicability of 'gain' as a core concept in biodiversity change, offering a more nuanced evidence base for interventions discussed in Hillebrand's recent work on tipping points.
proposals.novelty: While turnover is well-studied, explicitly inverting the analytical focus to quantify and characterize *beneficial* gains as a primary driver of change is novel. It challenges the implicit assumption that high turnover is inherently destabilizing or undesirable.

proposals.title: Topic Modeling of Ecological Communities: Revealing Latent Functional and Successional Structure in Biodiversity Time Series
proposals.motivation: Ecological community change is often summarized by aggregate metrics like species richness, which Hillebrand's work has shown can be biased and mask complex underlying dynamics. Understanding the non-random, multi-species patterns of community assembly and disassembly is crucial for predicting ecosystem responses to global change. Current methods struggle to identify these coherent, recurring groups of species that respond similarly to environmental drivers over time.
proposals.hypothesis: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) applied to species occurrence data will identify interpretable 'ecological topics'—recurrent groups of co-occurring species—that represent functional or successional modules. The temporal dynamics of these topic proportions will provide a more robust and mechanistically informative signal of community change than traditional richness or diversity indices.
proposals.method: We will treat each sampled community (e.g., per site and time point) as a 'document' and species presences/abundances as 'words'. Using established LDA algorithms (e.g., the `topicmodels` R package), we will perform unsupervised topic modeling on long-term and spatially extensive datasets (e.g., from the BioTIME database). Model selection (optimal number of topics, k) will use perplexity and semantic coherence. We will validate topics by correlating their distributions with environmental gradients and known species traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrative metric of community state based on species co-occurrence, moving beyond single-species or aggregate approaches. It will directly address the bias in richness trends by characterizing change as shifts in topic composition, offering a new way to test for thresholds and scaling rules in community stability, core themes in Hillebrand's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While topic models are standard in text analysis, their application to ecological community data to extract latent structure is novel. This approach fundamentally re-conceptualizes community dynamics as a mixture of persistent 'themes', offering a powerful alternative to analyzing individual species or aggregate metrics in isolation.

proposals.title: Decomposing non-stationary biodiversity change: An Empirical Mode Decomposition approach to scale-specific turnover dynamics
proposals.motivation: Biodiversity time series are inherently non-stationary, mixing seasonal, inter-annual, and long-term trends, yet dominant analytical methods often assume stationarity or impose rigid cyclic forms. This obscures the scale-specific drivers of change and complicates the detection of genuine directional shifts versus natural variability, a core challenge in assessing anthropogenic impacts. A flexible, data-driven tool is needed to disentangle these overlapping temporal signals without a priori assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) can adaptively separate complex temporal biodiversity turnover signals into physically meaningful, scale-specific intrinsic mode functions (IMFs), revealing that the variance and drivers of community change are partitioned distinctly across different temporal scales.
proposals.method: We will apply the EMD algorithm (Huang et al., 1998) to high-resolution time series of community composition (e.g., from plankton metabarcoding or benthic monitoring). For each site, a multivariate EMD variant will decompose a univariate diversity index (e.g., temporal beta-diversity) or a leading multivariate axis from an ordination into IMFs and a residual trend. We will correlate the energy of each IMF with environmental drivers (e.g., NAO, temperature, nutrients) specific to its characteristic timescale, using wavelet coherence and cross-correlation analyses to test for scale-specific driver-response relationships.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel methodological framework for diagnosing the temporal scales at which biodiversity change occurs, directly addressing calls to move beyond simple richness trends. It will yield a more nuanced understanding of how different environmental forcings imprint on community dynamics at seasonal, inter-annual, and secular scales, refining concepts of stability and tipping points.
proposals.novelty: While EMD is established in geophysics, its application to decompose ecological time series, particularly multivariate community turnover, is novel. It moves beyond traditional Fourier or wavelet transforms by being fully adaptive and local in time, making it uniquely suited for the irregular, non-stationary signals characteristic of real-world biodiversity data.

proposals.title: Testing compensatory mechanisms of community stability when species asynchrony declines
proposals.motivation: Ecological theory posits species asynchrony as a primary stabilizer of aggregate community properties like biomass. However, anthropogenic change can synchronize species dynamics, potentially weakening this mechanism. It is critical to understand if and how other stabilizing mechanisms, such as portfolio effects or compensatory species turnover, can buffer ecosystems when asynchrony is reduced, a key question for predicting biodiversity's role in ecosystem stability under global change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in communities experiencing declining species asynchrony, compensatory portfolio effects (via dominance shifts) and/or species turnover will become the dominant stabilizing mechanisms for maintaining aggregate community biomass over time.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term, spatially replicated time-series data of species-level biomass from aquatic communities (e.g., phytoplankton, zooplankton). Using variance partitioning approaches, we will quantify the relative contributions of asynchrony, portfolio effects (changes in species' relative contributions to total biomass), and species turnover (gain/loss) to temporal stability (mean/SD of total biomass). We will specifically test for shifts in the dominant mechanism along gradients of environmental change and declining asynchrony using generalized linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond simply correlating asynchrony with stability to mechanistically identify backup stabilizing pathways. It will provide a more nuanced framework for assessing ecosystem resilience, directly informing debates on thresholds and tipping points in biodiversity change by identifying functional redundancies in stability mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While asynchrony's role is well-established, this study explicitly tests for functional compensation among multiple, non-mutually exclusive stability mechanisms under realistic scenarios of synchronizing environmental change, integrating concepts from population, community, and metacommunity ecology.

proposals.title: Biomass stability as a universal predictor of ecosystem service reliability across scales and taxa
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem services are crucial for human well-being, but their reliable delivery under environmental change is uncertain. Pre-2026 research often correlates biodiversity metrics like species richness with service magnitude, but largely neglects the temporal reliability (i.e., stability) of service provision. This gap is critical because stable services are more valuable than fluctuating ones, yet we lack a general test of whether total biomass stability is the key underlying property for reliable delivery across diverse services.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal stability of total community biomass is a stronger and more consistent predictor of the reliability of multiple ecosystem services than static biodiversity metrics (e.g., species richness) or the stability of individual species. This relationship holds across spatial scales and ecosystem types.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term, spatially replicated time-series data of biomass and ecosystem functions (e.g., primary production, filtration rates) from aquatic and terrestrial networks (e.g., LTER, BioTIME). Using mixed-effects models, we will partition variance in service reliability (inverse of coefficient of variation) explained by: 1) biomass stability (CV of total biomass), 2) mean species richness, and 3) compositional turnover. We will explicitly test for scale-dependency by analyzing stability-service relationships at local, metacommunity, and regional scales using variance ratio approaches and spatial asynchrony metrics, building on Hillebrand's prior scaling work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, cross-system evaluation of a stability-focused paradigm for ecosystem service research, moving beyond snapshot diversity-function correlations. It will directly inform conservation and management by identifying whether stabilizing total biomass—rather than simply maximizing richness—is a more effective strategy for ensuring reliable benefits from nature.
proposals.novelty: While biomass stability is known to relate to ecosystem *function* stability, its primacy over other biodiversity facets for predicting the reliability of *multiple, distinct services* across scales is untested and non-obvious, given known context-dependencies and the tempting focus on richness or tipping points.

proposals.title: Interactive effects of eutrophication and drought on community stability: disentangling drivers of species asynchrony and biomass variability
proposals.motivation: Anthropogenic global change involves multiple, co-occurring drivers like nutrient enrichment, warming, and altered hydrology. Pre-2026 research often examines these drivers in isolation, yet their interactive effects on ecosystem stability—a key determinant of biodiversity's functional value—remain poorly understood. This gap is critical because non-additive interactions could lead to unexpected ecological thresholds and tipping points in community dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that eutrophication and drought interact synergistically to reduce community stability by disproportionately decreasing species asynchrony, leading to higher temporal variability in total biomass compared to the effects of each driver alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term time series of phytoplankton and zooplankton communities from lakes experiencing documented eutrophication and drought events, leveraging datasets like the North Temperate Lakes LTER. We will quantify stability metrics (e.g., temporal invariability of total biomass) and species asynchrony using the Loreau-de Mazancourt framework. The interactive effects will be tested using generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) that include interaction terms between nutrient concentration anomalies and drought indices (e.g., Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index), while controlling for site and time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, empirical test of how multiple stressors jointly alter stability, moving beyond single-driver studies. It will directly inform debates on the suitability of threshold concepts in biodiversity change by revealing whether interactions create abrupt stability losses. The findings will clarify if cross-scale stability patterns are primarily driven by individual species responses to interacting stressors.
proposals.novelty: While Hillebrand's prior work has cautioned against simplistic tipping point models and highlighted individual species dynamics, this proposal uniquely applies that critical perspective to the specific, understudied interaction between eutrophication and hydrological change, testing for non-additive effects on the core stability-asynchrony relationship.

proposals.title: Linking fertilization-induced stability changes in biodiversity to the provisioning of ecosystem services
proposals.motivation: Nutrient enrichment is a dominant global change driver, known to alter biodiversity and community stability. However, the consequences of these stability changes for the reliable delivery of ecosystem services like forage yield and carbon storage remain poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict and manage the functional impacts of anthropogenic nutrient loading on ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fertilization-induced shifts in community stability—specifically, changes in the temporal invariability of aggregate community biomass—will be directly linked to changes in the temporal mean and reliability of key ecosystem services.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term, multi-site grassland fertilization experiments (e.g., from the Nutrient Network) with data on species composition, biomass, and service proxies (e.g., aboveground biomass for forage, root biomass for carbon). We will calculate community stability (mean/invariance) and species-level stability contributions as per Hillebrand et al. (2018). Using structural equation modeling, we will partition the direct and biodiversity-mediated effects of fertilization on the mean and temporal reliability (coefficient of variation) of service proxies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative link between a fundamental ecological property (stability) and the provisioning of services under global change. It will move beyond simple richness-service correlations to show how environmental change alters ecosystem functioning through the dynamics of species and aggregate properties over time.
proposals.novelty: While stability-ecosystem function theory exists, empirical tests linking *environmentally-induced* stability changes to specific, measured services are rare. This explicitly connects Hillebrand's recent work on scaling stability from species to communities to a pressing applied question.

proposals.title: Trait-based network asynchrony: predicting community stability under nutrient enrichment from functional composition
proposals.motivation: Nutrient enrichment is a dominant global change driver, but its impact on community stability is poorly predictable. While asynchrony among species is a key stabilizer of aggregate properties like biomass, we lack a mechanistic framework to forecast how nutrient-induced shifts in functional traits alter species interactions and, consequently, asynchrony. This gap limits our ability to move beyond descriptive richness-stability relationships to predictive models grounded in ecological mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nutrient-induced changes in community-wide functional trait distributions will reconfigure interaction networks, and that the resulting changes in competitive asymmetry and niche overlap will be primary determinants of the direction and magnitude of shifts in species asynchrony.
proposals.method: We will integrate long-term time-series data from aquatic mesocosm experiments (e.g., from the Jena Experiment or lake fertilization studies) with trait databases. Using a generalized Lotka-Volterra framework, we will parameterize interaction strengths as a function of pairwise trait distances (e.g., body size, nutrient uptake affinity) and environmental filtering from nutrients. We will then simulate community dynamics under nutrient gradients, quantifying asynchrony via the community-wide temporal covariance of species biomasses. Model validation will use cross-validation on held-out time-series segments and comparison to empirical stability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a trait-based mechanistic model to predict stability outcomes of nutrient change, directly addressing calls to understand the drivers of spatial scaling of stability. It will move the field beyond the debate on thresholds and tipping points by offering a continuous, process-based prediction of stability change, highlighting which trait shifts most critically alter asynchrony.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based approaches are common, their integration into dynamic network models to explicitly predict asynchrony—a key stability component—under environmental change is novel. This bridges Hillebrand's work on spatial scaling of stability and bias in time-series with emerging network theory, moving from pattern description to mechanistic forecasting.

proposals.title: Rapid Trait Evolution Modulates Community Asynchrony and Stability Across Scales Under Eutrophication
proposals.motivation: Eutrophication is a major driver of biodiversity change, but its long-term effects are typically assessed using static species identities. Recent work shows rapid evolution can alter species traits within ecological timescales, potentially modifying species interactions and stability. However, we lack a framework to integrate this microevolutionary change into models of community dynamics and asynchrony across spatial scales, a key gap given Hillebrand's findings on scaling and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid trait evolution under eutrophication, by altering species' environmental responses and competitive abilities, will fundamentally modify the temporal asynchrony among species and sites, thereby either dampening or amplifying the destabilizing effects of nutrient enrichment on metacommunity biomass stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a trait-based, eco-evolutionary metacommunity model. Species' population dynamics will follow a modified Lotka-Volterra framework where key traits (e.g., R*, nutrient uptake affinity) are allowed to evolve via a quantitative genetics approach. We will simulate long-term time series under varying eutrophication gradients. Using variance ratio approaches and scaling analyses, we will partition stability into local vs. regional components and asynchrony contributions, comparing scenarios with evolution enabled vs. disabled. The analysis will specifically track how trait change alters the scaling of stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic link between microevolution and macroecological stability patterns, moving beyond descriptive richness trends. It will generate testable predictions on when evolution may buffer or exacerbate anthropogenic impacts on ecosystem functioning, directly informing debates on tipping points and the interpretation of biodiversity time series.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based ecology and rapid evolution are studied separately, their joint effect on the spatial scaling of stability is unexplored. This integrates Hillebrand's expertise in scaling, stability, and bias in time series with evolutionary dynamics, offering a non-obvious explanation for observed community trajectories under stress.

proposals.title: Decoupling community and species responses: Testing the masking effect of co-limitation in multi-nutrient enrichment experiments
proposals.motivation: Community-level metrics like total biomass or aggregate nutrient use efficiency often show saturating responses to enrichment, interpreted as co-limitation. However, this apparent stability may mask critical, divergent species-level shifts in resource use and growth, a dynamic overlooked in biodiversity-change research. This gap is critical because it risks misidentifying ecosystem stability and resilience, a core concern in Hillebrand's work on scaling and tipping points.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that community-level co-limitation signatures (e.g., constant biomass ratios) emerge not from uniform species responses, but from the compensatory dynamics of species with divergent, specialized nutrient limitations.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing long-term experimental data (e.g., from freshwater mesocosms or phytoplankton chemostats) with multi-nutrient gradients (N, P, Si). For each species, we will fit hierarchical Monod growth models to estimate species-specific half-saturation constants and limitation thresholds. Community-level co-limitation will be quantified via yield-based enrichment plots. We will then use variance partitioning and null models to test if community-level metrics can be predicted from the aggregate of species-specific models or if they mask significant species-level divergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework to disentangle community- and species-level drivers of biodiversity change under resource alteration. It will challenge the interpretation of community-level stability by revealing hidden compensatory dynamics, directly informing debates on thresholds and the scaling of stability from individuals to ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While co-limitation is well-studied at the community level, explicitly testing it as a statistical emergent property masking divergent species niches is novel. This bridges physiological ecology with community scaling, moving beyond Hillebrand's documented bias in richness trends to reveal hidden functional reorganization.

proposals.title: Network architecture of nutrient limitation: linking stoichiometric imbalances to community stability and biodiversity change
proposals.motivation: Nutrient limitation fundamentally structures communities, but its dynamics are often inferred from aggregate ratios (e.g., N:P) or single-species responses, neglecting the network of interspecific resource flows. This gap limits our ability to predict how biodiversity change, driven by eutrophication or nutrient depletion, affects ecosystem functioning and stability. A network perspective is urgently needed to move beyond simple thresholds and understand the pathways through which nutrient imbalances propagate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the architecture of nutrient flow networks—specifically their modularity and connectance—determines the resistance of community biomass stability to stoichiometric imbalances, and that shifts in this architecture explain observed biases in biodiversity time series under nutrient change.
proposals.method: We will construct nutrient flow networks for well-documented aquatic communities (e.g., from the Baltic Sea or lake mesocosm studies) using pre-2026 interaction data (diet, excretion, uptake). Nodes represent species/pools, weighted edges represent major nutrient (N, P, Si) fluxes. We will analyze network properties (e.g., using igraph in R) including modularity, node centrality, and link distribution. These metrics will be correlated with long-term time-series data on species richness, biomass stability, and environmental nutrients to test for architectural controls on community responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic framework linking stoichiometry to community dynamics, moving from phenomenological ratios to interactive pathways. It will directly inform debates on tipping points in biodiversity change by identifying network features that confer resilience or sensitivity to nutrient shifts.
proposals.novelty: While network ecology has focused on trophic or competitive interactions, applying it explicitly to multi-nutrient flows within a stoichiometric framework is novel. This integrates Hillebrand's expertise in biodiversity scaling and stability with metabolic and nutrient-based theories of ecology.

proposals.title: Integrating Empirical Nutrient Co-limitation Types into Global Vegetation Models to Reduce Uncertainty in Biogeochemical Projections
proposals.motivation: Global Earth System Models (ESMs) are critical for projecting climate feedbacks, but their representation of nutrient limitation on primary production is simplistic, often assuming single-nutrient (e.g., N or P) limitation. Pre-2026 empirical synthesis shows widespread and diverse co-limitation (simultaneous, serial, colimitation) across ecosystems. This disconnect introduces major uncertainty in modeled carbon cycle responses to environmental change.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating empirically derived, spatially explicit co-limitation types into the vegetation components of ESMs will significantly alter and likely reduce the projected uncertainty in global net primary production (NPP) under future climate and deposition scenarios compared to models using fixed, single-nutrient limitation schemes.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize a global geospatial database of empirical co-limitation types (N, P, N&P, etc.) from pre-2026 meta-analyses and trait data, linking types to environmental drivers (climate, soil, N deposition). This database will inform a probabilistic parameterization module for the JSBACH or LPJ-GUESS vegetation model. The module will assign co-limitation types dynamically across grid cells based on environmental covariates, modifying the model's core stoichiometric and uptake algorithms to reflect the specific limitation type (e.g., implementing Liebig's law for serial, multiplicative effects for simultaneous). Model ensembles with fixed and dynamic co-limitation will be compared under CMIP6 scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic integration of empirical co-limitation ecology into global-scale biogeochemical models. It will quantify the sensitivity of carbon cycle projections to nutrient limitation representation and produce a novel, testable global map of probable co-limitation regimes for future field validation.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of nutrients in ESMs is recognized, the explicit, data-driven representation of co-limitation *types* as dynamic emergent properties, rather than fixed regional parameters, is a novel synthesis of community ecology and global modeling not yet implemented pre-2026.

proposals.title: Experimental Disruption of Direct Pathways Reveals the Causal Strength of Indirect Interactions in Structuring Biodiversity
proposals.motivation: Indirect interactions are hypothesized to be dominant in structuring communities, but evidence remains largely correlational, derived from observational time series and network models. This limits mechanistic understanding and the ability to predict community responses to anthropogenic change. An experimental approach that isolates and manipulates specific pathways is needed to move from pattern description to causal attribution of interaction strengths.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted disruption of a key direct interaction pathway (e.g., plant-plant competition) will propagate through the network, causing community-wide shifts in abundance and dominance that are disproportionately large relative to the direct effect, thereby demonstrating the causal primacy of indirect interactions.
proposals.method: We will establish replicated mesocosm communities (e.g., aquatic or grassland) and apply a physical (root barrier) or chemical (signal inhibitor) treatment to disrupt a hypothesized strong direct interaction. High-resolution monitoring pre- and post-disruption will use eDNA metabarcoding for multi-trophic composition, stable isotope analysis (13C, 15N) for trophic links, and automated sensors for micro-environment. Causal inference will employ Structural Causal Models (SCMs) and Difference-in-Differences frameworks, using the treatment as an instrumental variable to quantify the strength and propagation of indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental, causal evidence for the strength of indirect interactions in a whole-community context. It directly addresses Hillebrand's critique of tipping point concepts by quantifying the continuous, network-mediated nature of change. The integrated methodological framework will set a new standard for moving from observational to mechanistic biodiversity research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit combination of targeted pathway disruption with formal causal inference models, moving beyond the statistical analysis of observational time series that dominate pre-2026 research. This approach tests the causality of network dominance directly, rather than inferring it from correlation or stability metrics.

proposals.title: Indirect Contagion of Dominance: Applying Complex Network Models to Understand Community-Level Biodiversity Change
proposals.motivation: Ecological dominance is typically attributed to direct species interactions like competition or predation, yet many systems are structured by strong indirect effects and facilitation. This gap limits our ability to predict biodiversity change, especially in the context of global change where indirect pathways may amplify or buffer species losses. Pre-2026 work, including Hillebrand's on biased richness trends and the scaling of stability, highlights the need for frameworks that move beyond pairwise dynamics to explain community-level patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: Dominance in ecological communities can propagate as a complex contagion through networks of indirect interactions, whereby a species' likelihood of becoming dominant is a threshold function of the dominance states of its indirectly connected neighbors. This indirect spread, mediated by network topology, is a primary driver of observed dominance structures and stability in facilitative or engineered environments.
proposals.method: We will adapt stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) and threshold models from social network epidemiology to ecological interaction networks. Using pre-2026 longitudinal biodiversity data (e.g., from aquatic communities), we will infer latent networks of potential indirect facilitation. Model parameterization will test if dominance state changes in a focal species are better predicted by the states of indirectly connected species (complex contagion) than by direct interaction strengths. Analyses will focus on how network features like clustering and centrality modulate the spread of dominance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic model for the emergence of dominance that integrates indirect effects, directly addressing critiques of tipping point oversimplification. It will yield testable predictions about how network structure influences community stability and the scaling of biodiversity change, bridging individual species dynamics and system-level properties.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by explicitly treating dominance as a property that spreads contagiously through indirect interaction networks, a conceptual shift from direct interaction frameworks. It applies well-established social network algorithms (SAOMs, threshold models) to a core ecological problem in a non-obvious way.

proposals.title: Decomposing Diversity: An Information-Theoretic Test of the Complementarity of Dominance and Evenness
proposals.motivation: Dominance and evenness are foundational, often implicitly assumed to be complementary components of species diversity, summing to describe a community's structure. This assumption underpins the interpretation of many biodiversity metrics and their responses to environmental change. However, this presumed complementarity has never been rigorously tested, potentially leading to misinterpretations of biodiversity change, especially in the context of non-linear community shifts and scaling issues highlighted in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard indices of dominance (e.g., Simpson's D) and evenness (e.g., Pielou's J) are not perfect complements, but instead share conceptual and statistical variance, failing to provide mutually exclusive information about total diversity (Shannon's H').
proposals.method: We will adapt the Partial Information Decomposition (PID) framework from information theory, which quantifies unique, shared (redundant), and synergistic information among variables. Treating Shannon's H' as the total 'information', we will mathematically derive the expected relationships between H', dominance, and evenness under perfect complementarity. Using extensive empirical time-series and spatial datasets (e.g., from prior aquatic community stability work), we will compute the statistical variance uniquely explained by each component versus their shared variance, testing for significant deviations from the theoretical expectation using variance partitioning and regression analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative test of a core assumption in biodiversity measurement, clarifying the conceptual independence of key diversity components. It will offer a novel analytical framework for diagnosing the drivers of biodiversity change, moving beyond tempting but potentially misleading threshold narratives by precisely decomposing change into its constituent parts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a sophisticated information-theoretic decomposition, common in signal processing and neuroscience, to a foundational ecological question. This moves beyond correlation-based analyses to formally test for informational redundancy and synergy between diversity components, a non-obvious approach given the field's historical reliance on ratio-based indices.

proposals.title: Decoupling dominance and evenness: A test of their functional complementarity in driving community stability and ecosystem function
proposals.motivation: Biodiversity-ecosystem function (BEF) research and stability theory often treat evenness and dominance as complementary, assuming that a community's evenness index (e.g., Pielou's J) fully captures the information lost when using a dominance index (e.g., Simpson's D). However, recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues highlights that individual species and site dynamics drive stability patterns, suggesting that simple summary metrics may obscure critical community aspects. If dominance and evenness are not perfect complements, their independent dynamics under environmental change could lead to biased predictions of ecosystem responses.
proposals.hypothesis: Dominance and evenness metrics are not mathematically or ecologically complementary; they capture distinct and independent aspects of community structure that respond differently to environmental drivers and have unique effects on ecosystem stability and function.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term, spatially replicated time series of aquatic communities (e.g., phytoplankton, zooplankton) from pre-existing networks. Using a variance partitioning framework, we will dissect the independent and shared contributions of temporal changes in dominance (Simpson's dominance) and evenness (Pielou's J) to the stability of aggregate community biomass and total resource use. We will employ state-space models to track their dynamics separately and use structural equation modeling to test for divergent causal pathways linking environmental drivers to each metric and, subsequently, to ecosystem properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical test of a foundational assumption in community ecology, clarifying whether these ubiquitous metrics should be used interchangeably as 'two sides of the same coin'. It will advance the conceptual framework for analyzing biodiversity change by identifying when and why dominance and evenness decouple, offering more nuanced indicators for ecosystem management.
proposals.novelty: While the mathematical relationship is known, the ecological independence of these metrics in dynamic, real-world systems is untested. This directly challenges an implicit assumption in much BEF and macroecological work, aligning with Hillebrand's recent focus on moving beyond simple richness trends and understanding the drivers of stability.

proposals.title: Dominance as a double-edged sword: Quantifying the effects of evenness on compositional stability and invasibility in multi-trophic communities
proposals.motivation: Understanding the drivers of community invasibility is critical for predicting biodiversity change. Pre-2026 theory suggests dominance (low evenness) can both inhibit invasion via resource pre-emption and facilitate it by reducing stabilizing niche differences, but empirical tests across systems are rare. This gap limits our ability to forecast how anthropogenic changes, which often alter dominance structures, affect ecosystem susceptibility to invasions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between dominance and invasibility is non-linear and system-dependent, mediated by the scaling of compositional stability. Specifically, we posit that high dominance initially increases invasibility by reducing stability but that extreme dominance in low-diversity systems can decrease invasibility due to biotic resistance from a few robust competitors.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term time-series data (e.g., from the BioTIME database) for aquatic and terrestrial communities, calculating per-site dominance (e.g., Berger-Parker index), compositional stability (e.g., temporal community dissimilarity), and invasibility (as the colonization rate and success of non-native species). Using structural equation modeling, we will test for direct and indirect (via stability) effects of dominance on invasibility across systems. Threshold models (e.g., piecewise regression) will be used to identify potential non-linearities in these relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative link between dominance, temporal dynamics, and invasion ecology, moving beyond simple richness-invasibility paradigms. It will directly inform the debate on thresholds in biodiversity change by testing if dominance levels serve as actionable indicators for invasion risk across different community types.
proposals.novelty: While dominance is a recognized community metric, its explicit role in mediating invasibility via temporal stability is under-explored empirically. The novel integration of time-series stability metrics with invasion data across systems to test for non-linear and indirect pathways represents a significant advance over static, richness-focused studies.

proposals.title: From Dominance Contests to Network Resilience: A Coevolutionary Framework Linking Individual Strategy to Community Persistence
proposals.motivation: Understanding how biodiversity change affects ecosystem stability requires moving beyond static richness metrics to the dynamics of species interactions. Pre-2026 work, including Hillebrand's, highlights that individual and site-level dynamics are crucial for scaling stability, yet mechanistic links between adaptive behaviors, interaction rewiring, and network-level resilience remain poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict how dominance hierarchies and behavioral plasticity, common in nature, buffer or amplify environmental change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ecosystems where agents adaptively rewire interactions based on dominance contest outcomes (e.g., forming coalitions or avoiding conflict) will exhibit greater structural and dynamic resilience to perturbations than those with fixed interaction topologies or non-strategic responses.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model where nodes represent strategic agents (species/individuals) with traits (e.g., competitive ability) and behavioral strategies (submission, avoidance, coalition). Interactions are modeled as edges in an adaptive network, with payoffs determined by evolutionary game theory (e.g., Hawk-Dove games). The core algorithm co-evolves strategy updates (via replicator dynamics) and network rewiring (based on fitness and dominance outcomes) in discrete time steps. We will simulate environmental perturbations and measure resilience through recovery of biomass ratios and temporal stability of richness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based model linking micro-scale behavioral adaptations to macro-scale ecosystem stability, directly addressing calls to move beyond simple tipping point narratives. It will generate testable predictions about how specific behavioral syndromes (e.g., coalition-forming) influence the scaling of stability from individuals to communities.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive networks and game theory exist separately, their tight integration to model coevolution of behavior and interaction structure in an ecological dominance context is novel. This approach moves beyond describing network topology to simulating its generative rules from first principles of individual fitness.

proposals.title: Quantifying systemic risk in ecological networks: Applying power grid stability metrics to assess dominance effects on community persistence
proposals.motivation: Understanding how biodiversity change affects ecosystem stability is a core challenge in ecology. Recent work, including Hillebrand's, highlights that simplistic thresholds and richness trends may be misleading, and that individual species dynamics are key. A quantitative framework is needed to assess the systemic risk posed by the loss of dominant species, which can trigger cascading secondary extinctions and destabilize entire interaction webs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ecological interaction networks can be rigorously assessed for systemic stability using adapted power grid metrics, where the loss of a dominant species (analogous to a critical generator) will have predictable, outsized effects on network persistence, quantifiable via eigenvalue-based resilience and N-1 contingency analysis.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic Lotka-Volterra-type interaction networks from empirical time-series data (e.g., from Hillebrand's aquatic community studies). We adapt the N-1 contingency criterion by sequentially simulating the removal of high-biomass or high-centrality 'dominant' nodes. System stability will be quantified by calculating the leading eigenvalue of the community matrix (Jacobian) at equilibrium, a direct analog to small-signal stability analysis in power systems. We will simulate perturbations to track cascading secondary extinctions and measure the network's return time to a functional state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for assessing the systemic risk of biodiversity loss, moving beyond descriptive richness metrics. It will directly test concepts of ecological dominance and critical nodes, offering a mechanistic link between individual species dynamics and whole-network stability, thereby informing conservation prioritization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal cross-disciplinary analogy, applying well-established engineering stability algorithms (N-1, eigenvalue analysis) to ecological dominance networks—a method not previously used to quantify systemic ecological risk in this direct, computational manner.

proposals.title: Mapping the Flow of Functional Traits: A Circuit Theory Approach to Metacommunity Connectivity
proposals.motivation: Understanding how functional traits and competitive dominance spread across fragmented landscapes is central to predicting biodiversity change. Current metacommunity models often lack a spatially explicit, quantitative framework for trait flow that accounts for heterogeneous resistance in the matrix. This gap limits our ability to forecast shifts in community composition and ecosystem function under environmental change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that landscape connectivity for trait flow can be modeled using circuit theory, where key corridors and barriers to trait movement will predict spatial patterns of functional trait aggregation and community dissimilarity in metacommunities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Circuitscape algorithm, a pre-2026 standard in landscape genetics, to model trait flow. Resistance surfaces will be parameterized using pre-2026 environmental data (e.g., land use, temperature) and habitat quality metrics derived from species biomass and trait data (e.g., body size ratios). By treating local communities as nodes and the intervening matrix as resistors, we will simulate cumulative connectivity for trait movement and correlate these predictions with empirical measures of functional beta diversity and stability from long-term aquatic community datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for predicting how landscapes filter and channel functional biodiversity. It will directly address calls for better integration of spatial dynamics in stability research and offer a new method to identify landscape features that either promote homogenization or maintain local trait distinctiveness.
proposals.novelty: While circuit theory is established in landscape connectivity and genetics, its application to the flow of traits and competitive dominance within metacommunities is a novel conceptual and methodological synthesis, moving beyond species-centric movement to model the transmission of ecological function.

proposals.title: Microbial Evenness as a Key Driver of Biogeochemical Stability: Testing the Dominance Framework in Aquatic Sediments
proposals.motivation: Microbial communities drive global biogeochemical cycles, yet biodiversity-ecosystem function (BEF) relationships at this scale remain poorly resolved. Pre-2026 work in macro-ecology, including Hillebrand's research on dominance and stability, suggests that shifts in species evenness, not just richness, critically affect ecosystem processes. Applying this dominance framework to microbes is essential to predict how anthropogenic changes in microbial community structure will impact fundamental processes like carbon mineralization and nutrient cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reduced evenness (increased dominance) in sediment microbial communities will decrease the stability (increase temporal variability) of biogeochemical process rates under environmental fluctuation, and that this effect is scale-dependent, being strongest at local (core) scales.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mesocosm experiment manipulating nutrient loading to generate gradients of microbial evenness in aquatic sediments, quantified via 16S/18S rRNA amplicon sequencing. Process rates (e.g., denitrification, respiration) will be measured repeatedly over time. We will apply the 'scaling of stability' framework from Hillebrand's recent work, using variance ratios to partition stability across spatial scales. The relationship between evenness indices (e.g., Pielou's J) and the temporal coefficient of variation of process rates will be tested using mixed-effects models, explicitly comparing local vs. aggregate community effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, dominance-focused bridge between macro-ecological theory and microbial BEF research. It will generate empirical thresholds for how changes in microbial evenness impact process stability, offering a more nuanced tool than richness alone for assessing ecosystem vulnerability. The results will directly inform concepts of tipping points in biogeochemical cycles under anthropogenic stress.
proposals.novelty: While evenness is recognized in macro-ecology, its explicit application as a predictor of microbial process *stability* across scales is novel. This moves beyond correlative microbial BEF studies by testing a specific, scale-explicit theoretical framework (dominance) derived from larger organisms on the functionally critical microbial world.

proposals.title: Linking Turnover Dynamics to Process Fluxes: A Mechanistic Early-Warning Framework for Predicting Dominance Shifts in Biodiversity Time Series
proposals.motivation: Current early warning signals (EWS) for regime shifts often rely on generic statistical indicators of critical slowing down, which lack mechanistic insight and can be confounded by environmental trends. This limits their utility for proactive conservation, as they cannot identify the specific ecosystem processes driving change or predict which species will be affected. A framework that connects subtle shifts in species turnover to alterations in underlying processes like nutrient cycling or disturbance is urgently needed to move from detection to prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific signatures in high-frequency species turnover rates are causally linked to changes in key ecosystem process fluxes, and that modeling the feedback between these inferred process changes and dominance trajectories can predict imminent shifts in community structure before traditional EWS are triggered.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term, high-resolution aquatic community datasets (e.g., phytoplankton, zooplankton) where biomass and process rates (e.g., nutrient uptake, production) are co-measured. We will develop process-specific turnover indicators by applying wavelet analysis and generalized dissimilarity modeling (GDM) to time series of species occurrence and abundance. These indicators will be used to train a state-space model that infers latent process fluxes from turnover patterns. The model will then project feedbacks onto dominance structure using interaction coefficients derived from functional traits and biomass ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, process-explicit EWS toolbox that identifies the drivers of impending regime shifts, not just their likelihood. It will advance ecological theory by formalizing the feedback between compositional turnover and ecosystem functioning. The framework will provide resource managers with predictive insights into which functional groups are at risk, enabling targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing turnover-based compositional EWS with the inference of ecosystem process dynamics, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic, predictive model. It directly addresses Hillebrand's critique of simplistic tipping point concepts by embedding early warnings within a dynamic framework of process-flux feedbacks.

proposals.title: Scaling of community stability: disentangling the roles of functional group dynamics and species-level asynchrony
proposals.motivation: Understanding how biodiversity stabilizes ecosystem functions across spatial scales is critical for predicting responses to global change. Recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues has shown that species and site dynamics are key drivers of spatial scaling of stability, but this framework remains rooted in species-level data. However, ecosystem functions are often more directly linked to functional traits and groups, and anthropogenic pressures frequently act on functional composition, not just species richness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling of community stability with area is more strongly predicted by the dynamics and dominance of functional groups than by the aggregate dynamics of individual species, and that shifts in functional group dominance represent a primary mechanism altering stability scaling in changing environments.
proposals.method: We will apply the spatial scaling of stability framework from Hillebrand et al. (2023) but shift the analytical unit from species to functional groups (e.g., defined by traits related to resource use, size, or feeding guild). Using pre-existing long-term time series of biomass (e.g., from aquatic monitoring programs), we will calculate stability (temporal invariability) and its components (population variability, asynchrony) for functional groups across nested spatial scales. We will use variance partitioning and linear mixed-effects models to compare the explanatory power of functional group versus species-level metrics for predicting stability-area relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will bridge the gap between biodiversity-stability and trait-based ecology, providing a mechanistic link between anthropogenic changes in functional composition and the scaling of ecosystem stability. It will offer a more management-relevant metric for assessing ecosystem resilience, moving beyond species counts to the dynamics of functionally important groups.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for stability are established, applying them explicitly to functional groups is novel. This directly tests whether functional dominance, a key outcome of global change, is a fundamental driver of stability patterns, moving beyond the assumption that species are the sole relevant unit.

proposals.title: Disentangling the drivers of biodiversity gradients: A test for the confounding of contemporary climate with historical and biogeographic legacies
proposals.motivation: Macroecological studies often attribute biodiversity patterns, such as species richness, to contemporary climatic gradients like energy availability or climatic favourability. However, these gradients are spatially autocorrelated with historical factors (e.g., glaciation history) and biogeographic constraints (e.g., dispersal barriers, evolutionary history), potentially leading to spurious inference. This confounding limits our ability to predict biodiversity change under global change, a core challenge in ecology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the apparent effect of contemporary climatic favourability on species richness is significantly confounded by, and not statistically independent from, the effects of historical and biogeographic factors across multiple taxonomic groups and ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze a global meta-dataset of community time series (e.g., from BioTIME) and spatial biodiversity databases, integrating richness, biomass, and species ratios with high-resolution climatic, historical (e.g., Last Glacial Maximum climate stability), and biogeographic (e.g., realm-specific species pools, geographic isolation) data. Using a multi-model inference framework within a Bayesian hierarchical modeling architecture, we will explicitly model the spatial covariance structure. We will employ variance partitioning and path analysis to quantify the unique and shared contributions of contemporary climate, history, and biogeography to observed richness patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative assessment of the independence of macroecological drivers, moving beyond single-factor explanations. It will directly inform more robust, process-based models for forecasting biodiversity change by clarifying which components of observed gradients are truly amenable to change versus which reflect historical constraints.
proposals.novelty: While the non-independence of drivers is often acknowledged, this study proposes a rigorous, global-scale test using integrative models on temporal and spatial community data to formally partition confounding, aligning with Hillebrand's critical stance on oversimplified interpretations of biodiversity change.

proposals.title: Testing the generality of biodiversity-stability scaling: Applying an integrative community-dynamics model to forest and marine ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues has developed an integrative model linking species, site, and community dynamics to explain stability scaling in grasslands and aquatic systems. However, the generality of this framework across fundamentally different ecosystem types, with distinct disturbance regimes, dispersal constraints, and life-history strategies, remains untested. Validating this model in forests and marine systems is crucial for determining whether universal principles govern biodiversity-stability relationships or if context-dependent mechanisms dominate.
proposals.hypothesis: The integrative model, which posits that the scaling of community stability is primarily driven by the dynamics of individual species and sites, will retain predictive power in forest and marine ecosystems, but the relative contributions of spatial insurance (dispersal) and portfolio effects will differ systematically due to contrasting environmental templates and species traits.
proposals.method: We will apply the existing hierarchical Bayesian state-space model framework, which decomposes community biomass time series into species- and site-level contributions to stability. Using long-term monitoring data (e.g., ForestGEO for forests, Reef Life Survey for marine), we will fit the model to estimate key parameters: species-specific population stability, spatial synchrony, and community-wide asynchrony. Model performance and parameter estimates will be compared directly to previous results from grassland and lake ecosystems using cross-validation and posterior predictive checks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical test of the universality of a leading mechanistic model for biodiversity-stability relationships. It will reveal whether core drivers of stability scaling are conserved across biomes or if model structures require ecosystem-specific modifications. The results will directly inform the debate on general vs. contingent ecological theory and guide the application of stability concepts in conservation.
proposals.novelty: While the model has been validated in specific systems, its application to ecosystems with vastly different structural and dynamical complexities (e.g., long-lived trees, pelagic dispersers) is novel and non-trivial, challenging the model's core assumptions about scale and synchrony.

proposals.title: Disentangling the temporal scaling of stability: How disturbance regimes modulate biodiversity effects on community biomass dynamics
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues (e.g., on spatial scaling of stability) highlights that community stability emerges from local species and site dynamics. However, temporal biodiversity change is often assessed with biased richness trends, overlooking how disturbances—key drivers of temporal heterogeneity—alter the diversity-stability relationship across timescales.
