Understanding this is critical to predict ecosystem responses to anthropogenic pressures like altered fire or grazing cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect of species richness on the temporal stability of community biomass is non-linear and scale-dependent, with intermediate disturbance regimes maximizing the stabilizing effect of diversity by promoting asynchronous species responses.
proposals.method: We will extend the individual-based, spatial scaling framework from Hillebrand's recent work to the temporal domain. Using long-term time-series data (e.g., from grassland or aquatic monitoring networks), we will fit generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) to quantify stability (mean/standard deviation of biomass) across nested temporal windows. Disturbance regimes (e.g., fire return intervals) will be incorporated as interacting covariates. We will use wavelet analysis and variance partitioning to separate the contributions of species richness, species identity (turnover), and disturbance to stability at different temporal scales.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a mechanistic framework linking disturbance ecology to temporal stability, moving beyond simple richness trends. It will yield testable thresholds for disturbance frequency/intensity that erode or enhance biodiversity's stabilizing role, offering concrete insights for ecosystem management under global change.
proposals.novelty: While diversity-stability relationships are well-studied, explicitly scaling them across continuous temporal grains and incorporating realistic disturbance regimes as a modulating factor—rather than a simple stressor—is novel. This integrates Hillebrand's focus on scaling and individual dynamics with his critique of simplistic tipping point narratives.

proposals.title: Trait-mediated assembly: integrating plant functional traits to mechanistically explain competition and facilitation signals in biodiversity time series
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues has shown that biodiversity time series often reveal complex, site- and species-specific dynamics that drive community stability and change. However, models explaining these dynamics often rely on phenomenological signals of species interactions (e.g., inferred competition/facilitation from co-occurrence or biomass ratios) without a mechanistic basis. This limits our ability to predict community responses to environmental change, a core challenge in pre-2026 ecology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating key plant functional traits (e.g., specific leaf area, plant height, seed mass) into dynamic community models will mechanistically explain the empirically observed signals of competition and facilitation, and significantly improve predictions of species richness and biomass trajectories over time.
proposals.method: We will extend existing dynamic community models (e.g., multivariate autoregressive state-space models) used in Hillebrand's recent work. Trait data from global databases (TRY) will be incorporated as species-specific parameters modulating interaction strengths. We will use a hierarchical Bayesian framework to estimate trait-mediated interaction coefficients, testing models with and without traits against long-term aquatic and terrestrial plant community time series. Model selection will be based on Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) and predictive accuracy for out-of-sample richness and biomass.
proposals.contribution: This work will move biodiversity change research from describing patterns to mechanistically understanding the drivers of species coexistence and turnover. It will provide a trait-based framework to assess when and why communities tip towards alternative states, directly addressing recent critiques about tipping point concepts. The resulting models will be more generalizable for forecasting biodiversity change under global change scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based ecology and time series modeling are established, their integration to explicitly mechanize interaction signals in dynamic community models for temporal forecasting is novel. This moves beyond static trait-environment filtering or phenomenological time series models dominant in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Bridging Observational and Experimental Realms: Validating a Dynamic Biodiversity-Ecosystem Function Model with Manipulative Data
proposals.motivation: Models derived from observational time series, which often show biased richness trends, are used to predict biodiversity-ecosystem function (BEF) relationships under global change. However, these models are rarely tested against data from controlled experimental manipulations, which directly establish causality. This validation gap limits confidence in model projections for ecosystem functioning under future biodiversity change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, process-based model calibrated on observational time-series data will accurately predict the direction and magnitude of ecosystem function (e.g., biomass production, stability) changes observed in independent biodiversity manipulation experiments.
proposals.method: We will use a hierarchical Bayesian state-space model, previously developed for observational community time series, that incorporates species-specific growth rates, environmental responses, and interaction coefficients. Model parameters will be estimated from long-term aquatic community datasets (e.g., phytoplankton, zooplankton). We will then run the model forward under the specific species richness and composition treatments of independent BEF experiments (e.g., Jena Experiment, Cedar Creek BioCON) and predict ecosystem function metrics. Predictions will be statistically compared to experimental outcomes using Bayesian R² and posterior predictive checks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative test of whether observationally-derived models can capture causal BEF relationships, directly addressing calls for stronger links between observation and experimentation. It will identify which model components (e.g., species identity effects, interaction strengths) are most critical for accurate prediction, thereby refining theory.
proposals.novelty: While models and experiments coexist in BEF research, a formal validation of a dynamic, multi-species observational model against manipulative experimental data is novel. It moves beyond comparing static statistical relationships to testing the predictive power of underlying ecological processes.

proposals.title: The architecture of productivity: Emergent biodiversity-ecosystem function relationships from interaction network structure
proposals.motivation: The relationship between species richness and ecosystem productivity (BEF) is a cornerstone of ecology, but its mechanistic basis remains debated. Pre-2026 research often treats species as independent, overlooking the role of interaction networks. Concurrently, Hillebrand's work highlights that biodiversity change is driven by individual species and site dynamics, and that richness trends can be biased, necessitating a deeper, structural understanding of community assembly and function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shape and strength of the productivity-richness relationship is an emergent property of the underlying trophic and non-trophic interaction network, with network modularity and interaction strength asymmetry determining the relationship's slope and saturation point.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic, multi-trophic ecological networks using empirical time-series data (e.g., from Hillebrand's aquatic community studies) to parameterize interaction strengths. We will employ a generalized Lotka-Volterra framework embedded within a network architecture, simulating community dynamics under environmental forcing. Using network metrics (e.g., connectance, modularity, node centrality) as predictors, we will statistically model the emergent BEF relationship via structural equation modeling (SEM) to disentangle direct richness effects from indirect network-mediated effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will move BEF research from a phenomenological, richness-centric view to a mechanistic, interaction-based theory. It will provide a novel framework to predict how anthropogenic changes that rewire interaction networks (e.g., species loss, invasions) will alter ecosystem functioning, directly addressing calls to move beyond simple richness trends and tipping point metaphors.
proposals.novelty: While network theory is applied to stability and food webs, its explicit use to derive the BEF relationship as an emergent network property is novel. This integrates Hillebrand's focus on individual species dynamics and spatial scaling with a structural network approach, moving beyond correlations to mechanistic causality.

proposals.title: Generality and context-dependency of biodiversity-stability relationships: A cross-ecosystem test of a scaling model
proposals.motivation: Generalizations about how biodiversity stabilizes aggregate ecosystem properties (e.g., total biomass) are often based on limited empirical datasets, risking context-dependency and bias. Hillebrand's recent work highlights that biodiversity time series can be biased and that stability scaling is driven by site- and species-specific dynamics. Testing conceptual models across a broader range of systems is therefore critical to distinguish universal principles from ecosystem-specific contingencies.
proposals.hypothesis: The scaling of community stability from species variances and covariances, as modeled in recent aquatic studies, is a general mechanism across ecosystems, but the relative importance of species richness, evenness, and synchrony in driving this scaling will vary predictably with ecosystem type and the nature of environmental change.
proposals.method: We will extend the variance partitioning framework from Hillebrand et al. (2024) to a curated meta-dataset of >30 long-term time series spanning marine, freshwater, and terrestrial ecosystems, focusing on biomass or abundance data. For each series, we will calculate species-level variances, species-species covariances, and aggregate community stability. We will then use mixed-effects models to test how ecosystem type (fixed effect) and environmental variability (moderator) influence the scaling parameters linking species-level statistics to community stability, explicitly testing for thresholds in these relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, cross-system evaluation of a core stability-scaling model, directly addressing calls to move beyond narrow case studies. It will identify which aspects of biodiversity-stability relationships are general versus context-dependent, offering more reliable guidance for conservation and management under global change.
proposals.novelty: While the scaling model is established, its systematic application across diverse real-world ecosystems to quantify context-dependency is novel, moving beyond theoretical simulations or single-system analyses to a robust empirical synthesis grounded in species- and site-level dynamics.

proposals.title: Temporal scaling of multifunctional redundancy: quantifying the insurance capacity of biodiversity under environmental change
proposals.motivation: The insurance hypothesis posits that biodiversity stabilizes ecosystem functioning, yet empirical tests have focused on single functions or static redundancy. In a changing world, understanding how the capacity for multiple functions (multifunctional redundancy) fluctuates over time is critical to predict ecosystem resilience. This directly addresses recent debates on the suitability of tipping points for biodiversity change and the need to move beyond simple richness trends.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multifunctional redundancy exhibits non-linear temporal dynamics, with thresholds in its response to environmental variation that are not predictable from changes in species richness alone. Specifically, we predict that redundancy for linked functions declines more sharply with disturbance than for independent functions.
proposals.method: We will analyze long-term time-series data (e.g., from freshwater plankton or benthic communities) where species biomass and multiple ecosystem functions (e.g., production, nutrient uptake, decomposition) are measured. For each time step, we will calculate multifunctional redundancy using a 'multifunctional richness' approach, quantifying the number of species required to support a high level (e.g., 80%) of multiple functions simultaneously. We will then model the temporal dynamics of this metric against environmental drivers (e.g., temperature, nutrients) using Generalized Additive Mixed Models (GAMMs) to identify non-linearities and compare its temporal scaling and variance (stability) to that of species richness and single-function redundancy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, time-explicit framework for quantifying the insurance value of biodiversity for multiple functions. It will advance the field by linking community dynamics to multifunctional stability, offering a more mechanistic basis for evaluating ecosystem resilience to anthropogenic change. The results will inform whether redundancy for different function bundles responds similarly to environmental forcing.
proposals.novelty: While redundancy is a static concept, its temporal dynamics are virtually unexplored, especially for multiple functions. This study uniquely integrates Hillebrand's expertise in temporal scaling of stability with the emerging framework of multifunctionality to test a core, but dynamic, component of the insurance hypothesis.

proposals.title: Testing the assembly rules of multifunctionality: A null model approach to disentangle biodiversity effects from random species co-occurrence
proposals.motivation: The relationship between biodiversity and ecosystem multifunctionality (EMF) is a central tenet of community ecology, but observed EMF is often confounded by environmental filtering and historical contingency. Pre-2026, most studies correlate observed richness with EMF, failing to separate the signal of non-random species assembly—potentially driven by complementarity or selection effects—from the outcome of random community assembly given the regional species pool. This limits mechanistic understanding of how biodiversity change affects ecosystem functioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed levels of ecosystem multifunctionality in natural communities will significantly exceed those expected from null communities assembled randomly from the regional species pool, indicating that non-random assembly processes enhance multifunctionality.
proposals.method: We will apply a null model randomization framework to long-term community time-series data (e.g., from phytoplankton or zooplankton monitoring). For each time point and site, we will calculate observed EMF using a threshold-based approach across multiple functions (e.g., productivity, nutrient uptake, stability). We will then generate null communities by randomizing species occurrence while preserving observed species richness and regional frequency, using algorithms like the 'independent swap' to maintain site-by-species matrix structure. The distribution of null EMF values will be compared to the observed value to calculate standardized effect sizes and significance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, process-oriented test of whether biodiversity-EMF relationships arise from assembly rules beyond chance. It will directly address Hillebrand's recent critique of simple richness-change correlations by introducing a null expectation. The results will clarify if anthropogenic changes in community composition are likely to push EMF beyond or below random assembly baselines, informing concepts of thresholds and tipping points.
proposals.novelty: While null models are classic in biogeography and coexistence theory, their application to test multifunctionality against a random assembly baseline is novel. This moves beyond describing patterns to explicitly testing a mechanistic hypothesis about assembly-driven functioning, integrating Hillebrand's expertise in scaling, stability, and bias in temporal trends.

proposals.title: Scaling of biodiversity-stability relationships to ecosystem multifunctionality in hyper-diverse microbial metacommunities
proposals.motivation: The relationship between biodiversity and ecosystem stability is a cornerstone of ecology, but its scaling to multifunctionality—the simultaneous provision of multiple ecosystem functions—remains poorly understood, especially in microbial systems. Microbial communities drive global biogeochemical cycles and are hyper-diverse, with functional redundancy and countless potential metabolic pathways, making them a critical but challenging testbed for biodiversity theory. Pre-2026 work, including Hillebrand's on spatial scaling of stability, highlights the need to move beyond simple richness-stability relationships and integrate community dynamics across scales to predict multifunctional stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling of stability with microbial diversity is steeper for multifunctionality than for single functions, and that this relationship is primarily driven by asynchronous dynamics among functionally distinct taxa rather than by overall species richness.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new high-throughput time-series datasets of aquatic microbial communities (e.g., from the Baltic Sea Time Series or experimental metacommunities), quantifying taxonomic and functional diversity via amplicon sequencing and metagenomic inference. Multifunctionality will be assessed using a suite of biogeochemical process rates (e.g., nitrification, carbon utilization, enzyme activities) measured in parallel. We will apply statistical frameworks from Hillebrand's recent work, using variance ratios to partition stability across spatial scales and functions, and employ structural equation modeling to disentangle the direct and indirect effects of richness, evenness, and functional composition on multifunctional stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for scaling biodiversity-ecosystem functioning theory to multifunctionality in the most diverse systems on Earth. It will directly test the applicability of concepts like thresholds and tipping points in hyper-diverse contexts, offering a more mechanistic understanding of how microbial community dynamics buffer or amplify environmental change across multiple functions.
proposals.novelty: While biodiversity-multifunctionality relationships have been explored in macro-organisms, applying rigorous scaling theory and stability metrics from community ecology to hyper-diverse microbial systems—explicitly testing for asynchrony-driven effects—is a significant and non-obvious advance. It moves beyond cataloging functional genes to dynamically link community structure, temporal variability, and integrated ecosystem performance.

proposals.title: Beyond Convexity: A Geometric Framework for Scaling Biodiversity Metrics with Realistic, Fragmented Species Ranges
proposals.motivation: Geometric frameworks for scaling biodiversity metrics, such as the species-area relationship (SAR), often implicitly assume species ranges are convex and contiguous. However, real-world ranges are frequently non-convex or fragmented due to habitat heterogeneity, dispersal barriers, and anthropogenic change. This mismatch between model assumptions and biological reality may bias predictions of richness change over space and time, a core concern in Hillebrand's work on biased biodiversity time series and spatial scaling of stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating realistic, non-convex range geometries will fundamentally alter the predicted scaling of species richness with area and lead to more accurate and less biased estimates of biodiversity change in fragmented landscapes.
proposals.method: We will extend existing geometric null models by representing species ranges as rasterized polygons derived from real distribution data (e.g., AquaMaps, GBIF). Using computational geometry libraries (e.g., Shapely in Python), we will calculate overlap among these complex shapes across nested sampling grids. We will compare richness-area curves generated from these realistic ranges against those from their convex hulls or other simplified geometric representations. The analysis will be applied to both simulated metacommunities and empirical aquatic community datasets to assess the effect on stability metrics and temporal trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic geometric null model for spatial biodiversity scaling, directly addressing the limitations of convexity assumptions. It will yield improved predictions for how richness changes with sampling scale in fragmented environments, offering a crucial methodological advance for interpreting field data and large-scale biodiversity assessments.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of range shape is acknowledged, its formal integration into geometric scaling theory for community-level metrics remains underdeveloped. This proposal moves beyond conceptual discussion to implement a practical, computational framework that explicitly quantifies the consequences of fragmentation for core biodiversity patterns.

proposals.title: Generality and context-dependence of biodiversity change: A cross-taxa and cross-ecosystem test of the piecewise quadratic model
proposals.motivation: Recent analyses using a piecewise quadratic model have revealed complex, non-linear trajectories of biodiversity change in specific datasets, such as phytoplankton. However, it remains unknown whether these patterns are general or contingent on specific taxonomic groups and ecosystem types. Given the global biodiversity crisis and the documented biases in time-series analyses, a systematic test across the tree of life and major biomes is urgently needed to understand the universal versus context-dependent signatures of change.
proposals.hypothesis: The piecewise quadratic model, which captures accelerating, decelerating, or regime-shift-like dynamics in species richness, will reveal fundamentally different temporal trajectories of biodiversity change across major taxonomic groups and ecosystem types, with these differences being predictable from intrinsic (e.g., generation time) and extrinsic (e.g., ecosystem connectivity) system properties.
proposals.method: We will apply the established piecewise quadratic modeling framework to publicly available long-term time-series data (e.g., from LTER, BioTIME, GBIF) for taxonomic groups not yet studied in this context, including terrestrial insects, soil microbes, marine invertebrates, and vertebrates. For each time series, we will fit the model using segmented regression (piecewise linear and quadratic terms) via a standard iterative algorithm to identify breakpoints and curvature, quantifying parameters like the timing and magnitude of shifts. We will then use meta-analytic mixed-effects models to test whether model parameters (e.g., breakpoint frequency, quadratic coefficients) systematically vary with taxonomic group, ecosystem type, body size, and environmental change gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive assessment of the generality of non-linear biodiversity change dynamics, moving beyond case studies. It will identify which taxa and ecosystems are most prone to specific change patterns (e.g., abrupt declines vs. gradual increases), directly informing conservation prioritization. The results will critically evaluate the model's utility as a general diagnostic tool for anthropogenic biodiversity change.
proposals.novelty: While the piecewise quadratic model has been applied, its systematic validation across the full spectrum of biodiversity is lacking. This proposal explicitly tests the model's transferability and links its parameters to biological and ecological traits, moving from pattern description to mechanistic understanding of differential vulnerability.

proposals.title: Disentangling the drivers of biodiversity scaling: A spatial point process framework for linking individual species dynamics to metacommunity patterns
proposals.motivation: Understanding the scaling of biodiversity patterns from local to regional scales is central to ecology, yet traditional approaches often rely on aggregated metrics like richness that mask underlying species- and site-level dynamics. Pre-2026 work, including Hillebrand's, shows that these individual dynamics are key drivers of stability and observed richness trends. Current null models for species distributions often fail to generate realistic spatial aggregation and co-occurrence patterns, limiting our ability to test mechanistic hypotheses about biodiversity change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial point process models, parameterized with empirical data on species' environmental responses and dispersal traits, can generate more realistic spatial occurrence patterns than traditional random placement models, thereby revealing how individual species dynamics scale to form observed biodiversity gradients.
proposals.method: We will fit inhomogeneous spatial point process models, specifically Log-Gaussian Cox Processes (LGCPs), to high-resolution species occurrence data from aquatic monitoring networks. Covariates will include local environmental conditions and a spatial random field to capture residual aggregation. Model fitting will use integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA) for Bayesian inference. We will compare the spatial structure (e.g., pair correlation functions) and resulting richness gradients from our LGCPs against those from null models of random placement and against observed data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic null model for biodiversity scaling that explicitly incorporates spatial structure and species-specific responses. It will advance the field by offering a rigorous method to test whether observed metacommunity patterns can be parsimoniously explained by the scaled-up dynamics of individual species, directly addressing recent findings on the primacy of species- and site-level processes.
proposals.novelty: While point processes are used in species distribution modeling, their application as a mechanistic null for testing biodiversity scaling theory, particularly in the context of Hillebrand's work on individual dynamics driving stability, is novel. This moves beyond phenomenological richness-area relationships to model the explicit spatial point patterns that generate them.

proposals.title: Individual perception shapes community assembly: A distance-decay framework for resource use similarity
proposals.motivation: Classical distance-decay relationships in community ecology describe how species similarity decreases with geographic distance, but this macroecological pattern emerges from individual behaviors. Pre-2026, a mechanistic understanding of how individual movement and perceptual ranges scale to community patterns is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict biodiversity change, as highlighted by critiques of tipping point concepts and the recognition of individual- and site-level dynamics as key drivers of stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the similarity in resource use or environmental perception between individual organisms follows a distance-decay relationship, and that the slope of this individual-level relationship predicts the strength of community-level distance-decay in species composition.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution tracking (e.g., acoustic telemetry for fish, GPS for birds) with stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ15N) to map individual movement and realized trophic niches in aquatic and terrestrial systems. For each individual, we will calculate pairwise dissimilarity in resource use (Euclidean distance in isotope space) against the geographic distance to all other tracked individuals. We will fit exponential decay models to these individual dissimilarity-distance matrices and compare their parameters (e.g., decay rate) to traditional species-level distance-decay curves generated from concurrent community surveys using Mantel tests and mixed-effects modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will mechanistically link individual foraging behavior to macroecological biodiversity patterns, moving beyond descriptive richness trends. It will provide a novel, process-based metric—the individual perceptual decay rate—to forecast how community similarity and stability respond to habitat fragmentation and climate change, directly addressing calls to focus on fundamental processes rather than aggregate thresholds.
proposals.novelty: While distance-decay is a cornerstone of biogeography, applying it to the similarity between individuals, rather than species assemblages, is a fundamental shift in perspective. It directly tests the individual- and site-dynamics emphasized in Hillebrand's recent work as the engine of larger-scale patterns.

proposals.title: Allometric scaling of energy use and dispersal drives spatial biodiversity gradients: a cross-ecosystem test
proposals.motivation: A core pattern in ecology is the increase in species richness from poles to tropics, but the underlying mechanisms remain debated. Pre-2026 theory proposes that body mass, via its allometric links to organismal energy use and dispersal ability, is a key determinant of these spatial gradients, but this has not been directly tested across multiple taxa and ecosystems. Resolving this is critical for predicting how climate change and habitat fragmentation will reshape biodiversity patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the steepness of species richness spatial gradients is positively related to the allometric scaling exponents of population energy use and negatively related to the scaling of dispersal ability, with these effects modulated by environmental temperature and connectivity.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze existing global datasets (e.g., BioTIME, GBIF) for multiple aquatic and terrestrial taxa, integrating species occurrence, body mass, thermal performance, and spatial environmental data. Using mixed-effects meta-regression models, we will quantify the relationship between the slope of local richness across latitudinal/longitudinal gradients and community-weighted mean body mass. We will then partition this relationship using path analysis (structural equation modeling) with estimated allometric scaling of metabolic rate (using established equations) and dispersal proxies (e.g., wing aspect ratio, larval duration, or observed range size).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, cross-ecosystem test of the allometric mechanisms proposed to generate large-scale biodiversity gradients. It will move beyond correlative patterns to quantify the relative importance of energy use and dispersal constraints. The results will inform mechanistic models forecasting biodiversity change under global warming and habitat loss.
proposals.novelty: While allometry is well-studied locally, its explicit, quantitative role in shaping macroecological patterns across scales is untested. This proposal uniquely integrates metabolic and dispersal theory with empirical gradient analysis to deconstruct a classic biogeographic pattern, directly addressing Hillebrand's recent critiques of simplistic trend analyses and tipping point concepts.

proposals.title: Contrasting continental templates: A multi-driver analysis of intercontinental differences in latitudinal diversity gradients
proposals.motivation: The latitudinal diversity gradient (LDG) is a fundamental macroecological pattern, yet its strength and shape vary markedly between continents and taxonomic groups. Pre-2026 syntheses often seek a single universal driver, but this fails to explain why gradients in, for example, North American trees differ from those in European marine benthos. Understanding these intercontinental discrepancies is crucial for predicting how biodiversity will respond to global change in different biogeographic contexts, moving beyond simplistic global averages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intercontinental differences in LDGs arise from the continent-specific relative importance and interaction of historical, climatic, and geometric (e.g., landmass area and configuration) drivers, rather than from the operation of a single dominant mechanism globally.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze existing, pre-2026, gridded species richness data for multiple taxa (e.g., trees, birds, fish) across North America, Europe, and Asia. For each continent-taxon combination, we will use spatial simultaneous autoregressive (SAR) models and variance partitioning to quantify the independent and joint contributions of contemporary climate (e.g., energy, water), historical climate stability (from paleo-climate models), and geometric factors (land area within latitudinal bands, topographic heterogeneity). The analysis will explicitly test for statistical interactions between drivers and continents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, multi-driver framework to explain why LDGs differ, directly addressing calls to move beyond monolithic explanations. It will contribute to Hillebrand's critical examination of tipping points and scaling by showing how large-scale patterns emerge from context-dependent driver interactions, not universal thresholds. The results will inform more regionally tailored biodiversity conservation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have mapped LDGs or tested individual drivers, the novel synthesis lies in the systematic, quantitative comparison of driver *suites* across continents for the same taxa, explicitly modeling interactions to reveal why a driver's effect is potent in one biogeographic template but muted in another.

proposals.title: Validating global biodiversity change syntheses with primary time-series data: A test for systematic bias in meta-analytical inference
proposals.motivation: Synthesis studies, particularly meta-analyses, are pivotal for understanding global biodiversity change but rely on aggregated, often heterogeneous, data. Hillebrand's recent work suggests meta-analyses may be systematically biased towards detecting increasing species richness. The emergence of large-scale, standardized biodiversity databases (e.g., BioTIME, PREDICTS, GFBio) now provides an unprecedented opportunity to empirically test the validity of conclusions drawn from the broader, more fragmented meta-analytical literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conclusions from major biodiversity meta-analyses regarding the direction and magnitude of temporal change in species richness will be inconsistent when tested against a curated subset of high-resolution, standardized primary time-series data, revealing a systematic bias in synthesis methodologies.
proposals.method: We will first compile key conclusions from influential pre-2026 meta-analyses on temporal biodiversity change. We will then extract corresponding primary data from global databases (BioTIME, PREDICTS), applying strict filters for standardization, time-series length, and spatial resolution. Using mixed-effects models and comparing effect sizes (e.g., log-ratio of richness change), we will directly test whether the aggregated database trends align with the meta-analytical consensus. We will specifically analyze if biases are related to study duration, ecosystem type, or spatial scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial empirical validation framework for biodiversity syntheses, directly addressing concerns about inference robustness. It will clarify whether global change narratives are built on potentially biased evidence and establish best practices for using informatics data to ground-truth synthetic conclusions.
proposals.novelty: While meta-analyses are routinely validated internally, a direct, large-scale external validation using emerging primary data infrastructures is novel. This moves beyond methodological critique to an empirical test of synthesis reliability, a key intervention in the debate about biodiversity thresholds and change.

proposals.title: The Biome-Specificity of Productivity-Diversity Relationships: A Standardized Re-evaluation of Hump-Shaped Patterns
proposals.motivation: The hump-shaped relationship between productivity and species richness is a foundational but contentious pattern in ecology, often derived from cross-biome syntheses. Recent work by Hillebrand and colleagues highlights that biodiversity time series are biased by environmental change, and scaling patterns are driven by local dynamics. This suggests that broad-scale patterns may mask critical biome-specific contingencies, limiting predictive understanding of biodiversity change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hump-shaped productivity-diversity relationship is not a universal pattern but emerges only in specific ecosystem types when standardized for spatial scale, environmental heterogeneity, and temporal dynamics.
proposals.method: We will compile a global dataset of species richness and biomass (as a productivity proxy) from pre-existing time-series and spatial surveys, strictly partitioned by biome (e.g., temperate lakes, grasslands). Using mixed-effects meta-regression, we will model the relationship within each biome, controlling for study duration, spatial grain, and environmental variability. We will explicitly test for threshold effects in the relationship using segmented regression and compare model fits (linear, quadratic, unimodal) using AICc, following Hillebrand's recent methodological focus on ratios and cross-scale analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, biome-contingent assessment of a core ecological pattern, directly informing debates on tipping points and biodiversity change. It will yield a refined framework for predicting how changes in productivity, driven by anthropogenic forcing, will affect diversity in specific contexts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond cross-biome generalizations to test the pattern's universality under strict standardization within biomes, integrating temporal dynamics and scale explicitly—an approach highlighted as lacking in current syntheses.

proposals.title: Decoupling of temporal scales: Disentangling the lagged and non-linear relationships between productivity and species richness in long-term ecological time series
proposals.motivation: The relationship between productivity and species richness is a cornerstone of ecology, but its temporal dynamics are poorly understood. Most studies examine static snapshots or short-term trends, potentially missing critical lags and scale-dependent responses that are essential for predicting biodiversity change in a non-stationary world. This gap is critical given pre-2026 concerns about biodiversity time series being biased and the need to move beyond simple tipping point narratives for managing ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the productivity-richness relationship is scale-dependent, with richness changes lagging behind productivity shifts at annual scales, but these lags diminish or change sign when analyzed over multi-year intervals, and that the coupling strength varies predictably with ecosystem type and initial diversity.
proposals.method: We will analyze publicly available long-term time series (e.g., from LTER, BioTIME) for aquatic and terrestrial communities where concurrent data on richness and biomass/productivity proxies exist. Using Dynamic Time Warping and cross-correlation analysis, we will quantify time lags between productivity and richness changes. We will then apply Generalized Additive Mixed Models (GAMMs) to test for non-linearities and scale-dependence by including interaction terms between productivity, temporal grain (1, 3, 5-year blocks), and ecosystem state. Residual analysis will isolate site- and species-level dynamics as per Hillebrand's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, temporally explicit framework for the productivity-diversity relationship, moving beyond equilibrium assumptions. It will directly inform how we interpret biodiversity trends in monitoring data and assess ecosystem stability under environmental change, offering empirical evidence on the temporal scales relevant for conservation interventions.
proposals.novelty: While productivity-richness relationships are well-studied spatially, a systematic analysis of their temporal scaling, explicitly testing for lags and non-stationarity across multiple ecosystems using long-term data, is novel. It integrates Hillebrand's focus on scale and bias in time series with a core ecological paradigm.

ideas.title: Process-Flux Early Warning Systems: Predicting Regime Shifts via Dominance-Process Feedback Signatures
ideas.description: This research fuses the study of ecosystem process effects on dominance with turnover-based early warning systems. It proposes to develop indicators that detect subtle, rapid changes in species turnover rates and use these patterns to infer specific alterations in underlying ecosystem processes (e.g., nutrient cycling, disturbance regimes). By modeling the feedback between these inferred process changes and the trajectory of dominance structure, the framework can predict which species or functional groups are likely to become dominant or collapse. This moves early warning beyond simple detection of compositional change to a mechanistic, predictive system that identifies the drivers of imminent regime shifts for targeted conservation intervention.

ideas.title: Testing Complementarity via Information-Theoretic Decomposition
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that dominance and evenness are perfect complements in describing ecological communities. Drawing an analogy from information theory and signal processing—where a signal's total information is decomposed into redundant and innovative parts—we propose a parallel analytical framework. The analogous solution involves using measures like mutual information to test if two components are truly complementary (mutually exclusive and summing to the total). Adapted to ecology, the research would involve taking a foundational diversity measure (like Shannon's H') as the total 'information' of the community. The goal is to mathematically and empirically test whether standard indices of dominance (e.g., Simpson's dominance) and evenness (e.g., Pielou's J) capture non-overlapping aspects of this total diversity, or if they share conceptual and statistical variance, thus challenging their presumed complementarity.

ideas.title: Applying Power Grid Stability Metrics to Ecological Dominance Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from power grid stability analysis to the ecological problem of modeling dominance effects on interaction web persistence. In power systems, networks are analyzed for stability using metrics like the N-1 contingency criterion and eigenvalue analysis of the system Jacobian to identify critical nodes and quantify resilience to shocks. The adapted research applies this framework by modeling ecological communities as interaction networks where dominant species are analogous to critical power generators or hubs. The method involves simulating the 'removal' of dominant nodes (N-1 analysis) and employing eigenvalue-based stability metrics to predict cascading secondary extinctions and the web's capacity to return to a functional steady state, thereby providing a quantitative tool for assessing the systemic risk posed by the loss of dominant actors.

ideas.title: Experimental Disruption and Causal Inference for Dominance in Indirect Interaction Networks
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond observation to experimentally test the dominance of indirect interactions. It uses targeted physical or chemical manipulations to disrupt hypothesized direct interaction pathways (e.g., root competition, chemical signaling) between key species in a community. The system then employs high-resolution, multi-trophic monitoring (e.g., eDNA metabarcoding, isotopic tracers, microclimate sensors) to track community-wide responses. Finally, advanced causal inference models are applied to the pre- and post-disruption data to quantify the strength of indirect effects and rigorously establish their role in structuring dominance hierarchies, providing mechanistic evidence rather than correlation.

ideas.title: Network Contagion Models for Indirect Dominance in Ecological Communities
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that dominance in ecological systems arises primarily from direct species interactions (e.g., competition, predation). Drawing an analogy from social network epidemiology, where influence and behavior spread through indirect network contagion rather than just direct contact, we adapt complex contagion models to ecology. The analogous solution from epidemiology uses stochastic actor-oriented models and threshold models to track how states propagate through latent ties and structural network features. The adapted research idea applies this framework to ecological dominance, modeling it as a property that spreads through indirect interaction networks (facilitation, shared enemies, engineered environments). The research would develop and parameterize models where a species' dominance is a function of the states of indirectly connected species and the topology of the facilitation network, moving beyond pairwise direct interactions to understand dominance in systems where indirect effects or facilitation dominate.

ideas.title: Adapting Empirical Mode Decomposition from Geophysics for Non-Stationary Biodiversity Signals
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics and climate science to the problem of decomposing temporal biodiversity signals. The analogous field uses Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD), a data-driven method for analyzing non-stationary signals like seismic data, to adaptively extract intrinsic oscillatory modes and trends. The adapted research idea applies EMD to time-series data of biodiversity turnover (e.g., from metabarcoding or long-term monitoring). This allows for the separation of complex, real-world ecological signals into their constituent temporal scales—such as seasonal fluctuations, El Niño/La Niña-driven cycles, and long-term directional change—without imposing rigid sinusoidal assumptions, thus offering a more flexible tool for identifying the drivers of community change across different time horizons.

ideas.title: Adaptive Coevolutionary Networks for Dominance-Driven Ecosystem Resilience
ideas.description: This redesigned system models ecological interaction webs as adaptive coevolutionary networks, where nodes represent strategic agents (e.g., species or individuals) whose behavioral responses and interaction links dynamically change based on dominance contest outcomes and fitness consequences. It employs a framework combining evolutionary game theory on networks with adaptive rewiring rules, allowing the network topology and agent strategies to co-evolve. This approach directly tests how different strategic adaptations (e.g., submission, avoidance, coalition formation) in response to dominance alter the network's structural and dynamic resilience, providing a mechanistic pathway from individual behavior to web-level persistence.

ideas.title: Applying Circuit Theory from Connectivity Science to Metacommunity Trait Flow
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from landscape connectivity science, where circuit theory is used to model animal movement corridors, to the problem of tracking traits and dominance in metacommunities. The analogous solution treats the landscape as an electrical circuit with varying resistance to movement. By adapting this, we can model the metacommunity landscape as a circuit where habitat types and environmental matrices have 'resistance' values to the flow of genetic information or competitive dominance. Applying circuit theory algorithms will generate predictions of cumulative connectivity for trait flow, identifying key corridors that facilitate spread and barriers that isolate communities and maintain local trait patterns, thereby providing a novel, quantitative method for landscape genetics in an ecological context.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Modeling of Dominance Regime Transitions
ideas.description: This research program develops a general, mechanistic theory to explain transitions between stable and dynamic dominance regimes across biological, social, and technological systems. It employs generative agent-based models and cross-domain case studies to identify the critical parameters and feedback loops that destabilize static hierarchies and sustain dynamic competition. The core output is a predictive framework that specifies the boundary conditions for dominance volatility, moving beyond observation to enable forecasting of regime shifts. This allows for the design of experiments and analyses to test hypothesized mechanisms against empirical data from ecology, economics, and organizational science.

ideas.title: Standardize turnover metrics for policy reporting using a clinical trials registry model
ideas.description: This research idea proposes adapting the solution from clinical research regulation, where standardized outcome reporting (via registries like ClinicalTrials.gov and frameworks like CONSORT) solved the problem of incomparable data for policy and meta-analysis. The analogous problem in biodiversity is the lack of standardized, mandatory reporting for turnover and dominance metrics, hindering cross-national policy assessment. The adapted solution is to design and advocate for an international 'BioTurnoverRegistry' framework. This framework would mandate that parties to agreements like the CBD submit biodiversity monitoring data using specific, standardized turnover and dominance indices in a consistent format, enabling transparent, comparable tracking of compositional change for global policy reporting and accountability.

ideas.title: Modeling Dominance as a Non-Equilibrium Phase Transition
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of dominance as a static property by drawing an analogy from non-equilibrium physics. In physical systems like convection cells or oscillating chemical reactions, ordered, seemingly stable structures emerge and persist only through constant energy dissipation—they are dynamic by nature. The analogous solution involves modeling these as dissipative structures, identifying control parameters that trigger phase transitions between dynamic states. Adapted to the study of dominance, this approach proposes modeling social, political, or ecological hierarchies as non-equilibrium systems. Dominance patterns (e.g., in animal groups, markets, or political coalitions) would be seen as structures sustained by continuous fluxes of resources, information, or challenges. The research would aim to identify the equivalent 'control parameters' (like rate of resource inflow or intensity of competition) and model the conditions under which the system undergoes a phase transition from one dynamic dominance regime (e.g., stable hierarchy) to another (e.g., cyclical leadership or chaotic contention), thereby reframing dominance as a property of system dynamics rather than of static actor attributes.
